title: Anomaly-Informed Confidence Calibration for Vision-Based Safety Prediction
abstract: 在自动驾驶赛车中安全部署基于视觉的控制器时，可靠的置信度估计至关重要——安全预测必须从摄像头图像中推导得出，但现代预测器在测试时遭遇数据分布偏移时会变得过度自信，带来安全隐患。我们识别出现有异常信号中关键的感知-动力学鸿沟：广泛使用的评分指标（如自编码器重建误差）能捕捉视觉异常，但无法感知动力学异常（例如执行器偏置、延迟），这类异常中图像看似合理而实际轨迹却在退化。为此，我们提出一种异常感知在线校准方法，无需重新训练任何模型组件，通过融合从世界模型中提取的两个互补异常评分——基于重建误差的感知评分与基于认知不确定性和控制流统计量的动力学评分，实现校准。基于融合后的评分，轻量级温度缩放校准器利用测试时数据增强技术，在保持标称条件性能的同时，选择性降低偏移状态下的过度置信。在物理DonkeyCar平台上，针对训练中未见过的四种真实异常协议（黑暗、模糊、执行器偏置、处理延迟）开展的实验表明，该方法在不修改基础安全预测器的前提下，将平均期望校准误差从0.184降至0.116，较最优基线提升37%。

Anomaly-Informed Confidence Calibration
for Vision-Based Safety Prediction
Zhenjiang Mao
1†
, Jiawen Wu
1†
, Gabriel Wagner
1
, Zhongzheng Zhang
1
and Ivan Ruchkin
1
†
First co-authors: equal contribution.
1
Trustworthy Engineered Autonomy (TEA) Lab,
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering,
University of Florida, Gainesville, FL 32611, USA
{z.mao, jiawenwu, gabriel.wagner, i.ruchkin}@ufl.edu
Abstract
Reliable confidence estimates are important for safely deploying vision-based controllers in autonomous racing, where safety predictions must be derived from camera images, yet modern predictors become dangerously overconfident under test-time distribution shifts. We identify a critical
perception-dynamics gap
in existing anomaly signals: widely used scores, such as autoencoder reconstruction error, capture visual corruptions but miss dynamics anomalies (e.g., actuation bias, latency), where images remain plausible while the trajectory degrades. To address this, we propose an
Anomaly-Informed Online Calibration
approach that, without retraining any model component, fuses two complementary anomaly scores extracted from a world model: a perceptual score from reconstruction error and a dynamics score from epistemic uncertainty and control-stream statistics. Based on these fused scores, a lightweight temperature-scaling calibrator leverages test-time augmentation to selectively reduce overconfidence under shift while preserving nominal-condition performance. Experiments on a physical DonkeyCar under four real-world anomaly protocols unseen during training (darkness, blur, actuation bias, processing latency) reduce average expected calibration error from 0.184 to 0.116, a 37% improvement over the best baseline, without modifying the base safety predictor.
I
INTRODUCTION
Reliable confidence estimates are key to deploying vision-based robotic policies in safety-critical settings, yet modern predictors often exhibit miscalibration and dangerously overconfident behavior under test-time anomalies
[
14
]
. The safety evaluator must therefore provide confidence scores that reflect the true likelihood of remaining safe. This confidence miscalibration is significantly exacerbated when encountering distribution shifts during deployment
[
6
,
17
]
. A key obstacle is what we call the perception-dynamics gap: standard anomaly scores operate entirely in observation space and are therefore blind to failures that originate in the dynamics-actuator bias, control latency, or unmodeled friction — where the image stream appears nominal yet the closed-loop trajectory silently diverges.
Standard post-hoc calibration methods, such as temperature scaling, rely on a single learned scalar and lose performance substantially under dataset shift
[
22
]
. Richer uncertainty quantification techniques (MC Dropout
[
5
]
, Deep Ensembles
[
13
]
) improve robustness but incur computational costs that conflict with real-time control, and treat all anomalies as a monolithic source of uncertainty without distinguishing perceptual from dynamics-driven deviations
[
21
,
25
]
.
Distribution shifts can corrupt the observation model (visual noise, lighting changes) or the dynamical model (actuation noise, unmodeled physics)
[
7
,
10
]
, as illustrated on the left of Fig.
1
.
Variational Autoencoder (VAE) reconstruction error
[
1
]
, a popular anomaly signal, quantifies deviation from the learned visual manifold but is blind to dynamics anomalies (e.g., actuation lag, frame-freezing) where images remain on-manifold, while the latent trajectory diverges from learned physics.
This motivates a calibration strategy conditioned on
disentangled
anomaly evidence spanning both perception and dynamics.

Our core idea is to let the calibrator observe
the kind
of anomaly and
how severe
it is, rather than relying on a single fixed temperature. Thus, we propose a real-time
anomaly-informed online calibration
framework for vision-based robotic safety prediction. Our method repurposes signals from an anomaly-unaware world model to construct two complementary anomaly scores: a perceptual score from VAE reconstruction error, and a dynamics score derived from epistemic uncertainty estimates and control-stream statistics.
We additionally apply lightweight
test-time augmentation (TTA)
to smooth predictions under visual shift.
These scores are then fed into a lightweight temperature scaling module, adjusting the effective temperature. Finally, confidence is adjusted in response to perceptual or dynamics anomalies, while nominal behavior is preserved otherwise.
The primary contributions of this work are as follows:
1.
An
online calibration framework
that fuses anomaly evidence from a world model with test-time augmentation to adjust safety confidence without retraining.
2.
A
disentangled anomaly scoring strategy
that separates perceptual and dynamics scores, closing the gap left by purely visual anomaly signals.
3.
A
physical DonkeyCar validation
showing that calibration trained on synthetic augmentations transfers to qualitatively different real-world corruptions, with a
37
%
37\%
reduction in calibration error over the best baseline across four unseen OOD protocols.
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section II establishes the formal problem setting. Section III details our anomaly-informed calibration framework. Section IV describes the experimental setup on a physical DonkeyCar platform and analyzes the results under four real-world distribution shift protocols. Section V reviews related work, and Section VI concludes with a discussion of limitations and future directions.

Anomaly Injection Protocols.
(1) Visual Anomalies:
We introduce environmental and sensor-level corruptions inspired by the ImageNet-C benchmark

V
RELATED WORK
Confidence calibration.
Post-hoc calibrators range from parametric methods (beta calibration, Bayesian Binning) to density-aware approaches; DAC
[
23
]
, which modulates temperature via KNN feature density, is the most related baseline.
Static methods degrade under drift
[
22
,
17
]
; multi-domain temperature scaling
[
25
]
explicitly leverages heterogeneity across multiple training domains to improve calibration robustness under distribution shift, outperforming standard methods on both in-distribution and OOD test sets, while MC Dropout
[
5
]
and Deep Ensembles
[
13
]
improve robustness at multi-pass cost.
Test-time augmentation improves calibration via stochastic input averaging
[
9
]
.
Our method differs by conditioning temperature on disentangled anomaly scores while keeping the base predictor frozen.
OOD detection and anomaly scoring.
The Maximum Softmax Probability baseline
[
11
]
and Mahalanobis distance in feature space
[
14
]
are standard OOD detectors; covariate and concept shift further challenge detection and calibration
[
21
]
, where covariate shift reflects style-level changes (
e.g.
, noise) and concept shift reflects semantic-level changes — each requiring distinct score functions for reliable OOD detection and confidence calibration; Feature Norm
[
26
]
and energy scores provide alternatives.
VAE reconstruction error
[
1
]
is widely used for visual anomaly detection.
Outlier exposure
[
12
]
trains against auxiliary outlier data, with later work synthesizing virtual outliers in feature space
[
3
]
.
Unlike standard detectors that produce binary flags, we use continuous anomaly scores to
quantitatively
adjust confidence, and apply outlier exposure to train a separate calibrator rather than the detector itself.
Complementary visual OOD signals include attention-map monitoring, vision-language semantic detection, KNN feature distance, and activation truncation
[
20
]
.
These, like our
ρ
\rho
, target the observation stream; our addition is a dynamics score
δ
\delta
for anomalies invisible to image-level methods.
Safety prediction and world models.
World-model-based safety prediction
[
15
]
and autoencoder misbehavior detection (SelfOracle
[
19
]
, later extended with ensembles) are the closest antecedents.
Our work builds on the VAE-RNN World Models paradigm
[
7
,
8
]
and the end-to-end driving approach
[
2
]
underlying DonkeyCar.
VI
DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION
We presented an anomaly-informed calibration framework that fuses TTA with world-model-derived perceptual and dynamics anomaly scores, reducing OOD-average ECE from
0.184
0.184
to
0.116
0.116
(
37
%
37\%
over the best baseline) on a physical DonkeyCar under four unseen anomaly protocols without retraining.
The ablation shows that
ρ
\rho
provides the dominant signal (
28
%
28\%
ECE reduction) since TTA already handles visual robustness implicitly, while
δ
\delta
adds cross-seed stability and stronger dynamics-anomaly detection.
Transfer from synthetic augmentations to real-world protocols supports Assumption
1
, though extrapolation beyond the training severity range remains a limitation.
Future work includes closed-loop safety integration, online calibrator updates, and conformal prediction for calibration guarantees.

preview_text:
Reliable confidence estimates are important for safely deploying vision-based controllers in autonomous racing, where safety predictions must be derived from camera images, yet modern predictors become dangerously overconfident under test-time distribution shifts. We identify a critical perception-dynamics gap in existing anomaly signals: widely used scores, such as autoencoder reconstruction error, capture visual corruptions but miss dynamics anomalies (e.g., actuation bias, latency), where images remain plausible while the trajectory degrades. To address this, we propose an Anomaly-Informed Online Calibration approach that, without retraining any model component, fuses two complementary anomaly scores extracted from a world model: a perceptual score from reconstruction error and a dynamics score from epistemic uncertainty and control-stream statistics. Based on these fused scores, a lightweight temperature-scaling calibrator leverages test-time augmentation to selectively reduce overconfidence under shift while preserving nominal-condition performance. Experiments on a physical DonkeyCar under four real-world anomaly protocols unseen during training (darkness, blur, actuation bias, processing latency) reduce average expected calibration error from 0.184 to 0.116, a 37% improvement over the best baseline, without modifying the base safety predictor.

Anomaly-Informed Confidence Calibration
for Vision-Based Safety Prediction
